threat_model.name: Financial Services Trading Platform STRIDE + MITRE Analysis
threat_model.methodology: STRIDE + MITRE ATT&CK for Enterprise + FINRA/SEC threat taxonomy

trust_boundaries.name: Public Internet
trust_boundaries.description: Trader browsers, mobile apps, external market data feeds

trust_boundaries.name: AWS Edge Layer
trust_boundaries.description: CloudFront CDN, WAF, ALB — TLS termination

trust_boundaries.name: Application Tier
trust_boundaries.description: Next.js, Express BFF, Node.js microservices, Go risk engine — EKS/GKE

trust_boundaries.name: Data Tier
trust_boundaries.description: Cloud Spanner, AlloyDB, Redis, BigQuery — private VPC

trust_boundaries.name: External Payment Services
trust_boundaries.description: Stripe, Plaid, clearing houses, market makers

threats.description: SSRF via Next.js Host header (CVE-2024-34351) allows attacker to forge server-side requests to GCP metadata endpoint (metadata.google.internal:80), stealing the Compute Engine service account token. Token provides access to Spanner trade ledger and Cloud KMS encryption keys. Full account takeover possible.
mitigations: Upgrade Next.js to 14.1.1 immediately (CVE-2024-34351 patch)
Strip and replace Host header at nginx/ALB before Next.js
GCP network egress filtering: block metadata.google.internal from Next.js
SSRF protection: allowlist outbound IP ranges for Next.js Server Actions
Rotate all GCP service account keys if compromise suspected

threats.description: Insider threat (rogue developer or compromised service account) modifies historical trade records in Cloud Spanner to falsify P&L, evade regulatory capital requirements, or manipulate performance metrics for SOX reporting. Spanner's global consistency makes tampered records appear legitimate.
mitigations: Spanner row-level Data Access audit logs with DATA_READ enabled
Immutable audit log export to BigQuery (append-only table)
Cryptographic audit trail: trade record hash chain (Merkle tree)
SOX ITGC: two-person approval for any Spanner schema or data changes
Read replicas for regulatory reporting — prevent write access from reporting path

threats.description: Man-in-the-middle attack on order data in transit between the order execution service and Cloud Spanner (no SSL enforced on AlloyDB). Attacker modifies trade quantities, prices, or counterparty identifiers in transit. Real-time risk controls would not detect manipulation as the tampered order appears valid.
mitigations: Enforce SSL=verify-full on all AlloyDB connections
HMAC signing of order objects at creation (verify at persistence)
gRPC mutual TLS between order service and risk engine
Order idempotency keys using cryptographic UUID (not Math.random())
Istio mTLS for all pod-to-pod communication

threats.description: Trade risk approval workflow lacks non-repudiation. Risk officer claims they did not approve a trade that exceeded VaR limits. Without cryptographic signatures on risk approval decisions, the firm cannot prove to FINRA/SEC that the approval was genuine. Audit reconstructability failure under FINRA Rule 4370.
mitigations: RSA-SHA256 digital signature on all risk approval decisions
Immutable approval log in Cloud Spanner (version history enabled)
MFA-enforced approval workflow — approval action cryptographically bound to FIDO2 key
CloudTrail + GCP Cloud Audit Logs export to WORM storage
OpenTelemetry trace correlation between risk request and approval event

threats.description: Kubernetes Secrets on EKS cluster are not envelope-encrypted with KMS. An attacker who gains access to the EKS API server (via the public endpoint) can enumerate all Kubernetes Secrets, extracting trading API keys, Plaid client secrets, AlloyDB passwords, and PCI-scope cardholder data environment credentials.
mitigations: Enable EKS envelope encryption with AWS KMS CMK
Disable EKS public endpoint — private endpoint only via VPN
Migrate secrets to AWS Secrets Manager + GCP Secret Manager via CSI driver
IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) instead of long-lived credentials
Kubernetes Network Policy: deny pod-to-kube-apiserver except ci/cd namespace

threats.description: Redis AUTH token not configured on ElastiCache. Any compromised container in the EKS VPC can connect to Redis and read/write trader session data. Attacker can insert a session token for a high-privilege trader account without authentication, then use that session to execute unauthorized trades.
mitigations: Enable ElastiCache Redis AUTH token immediately
Enable in-transit TLS on Redis (Encrypted=true in redis client config)
Restrict Redis security group: only EKS node security group can access port 6379
Implement session token HMAC validation in BFF layer (detect tampered sessions)
Redis MONITOR command disabled (prevents session enumeration by insider)

threats.description: WebSocket server for real-time trade price feed does not validate Origin header (CWE-346). A malicious website can open a WebSocket connection from a trader's browser to the trade feed server, subscribe to live order book data, and send trade execution commands — Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking (CSWSH).
mitigations: Validate Origin header against allowlist of known trading domains
Require authentication ticket (short-lived JWT) in WebSocket handshake URL
SameSite=Strict session cookies prevent automatic cookie inclusion
CORS policy restricting WebSocket to single-origin
WebSocket message signing with HMAC using session-derived key

threats.description: Compromised market data provider (third-party data feed) or malicious actor with feed access floods the ingestion WebSocket with high-frequency large messages. Node.js heap exhaustion (CVE-2024-22019 pattern) causes the market data service to crash, blinding the risk engine during active market conditions and potentially causing erroneous risk approvals.
mitigations: WebSocket message size limit: 1MB per message (market data is typically <50KB)
Per-connection rate limiting: max 10,000 messages/minute
Circuit breaker: auto-switch to backup feed if primary exceeds limits
Node.js --max-old-space-size=4096 + OOM container restart policy
Kubernetes HPA + PodDisruptionBudget for market data pods

threats.description: Algorithmic trading DoS: A competitor or malicious actor floods the trading API with high-frequency spoofed order submissions (order stuffing), overwhelming the risk engine and degrading legitimate order processing. During peak market volatility, this DoS results in missed trading opportunities and potential best-execution regulatory violations (MiFID-II Art.27).
mitigations: WAF rate-based rules: max 1000 requests/5-minutes per trader IP
Application-level order rate limits: max 10 orders/second per account
Order throttling with exponential backoff communicated to clients
AWS Shield Advanced for L7 DDoS protection
FINRA SEC Rule 15c3-5 kill switch: auto-halt if order rate 10x baseline

threats.description: Long-lived IAM access key for service user 'finserv-app-svc' (347 days without rotation) is compromised via credential dumping from EKS pod environment variables. Attacker uses key to access PCI-scoped S3 buckets, CloudTrail logs, and modify audit records — covering tracks for insider trading activity.
mitigations: Migrate from IAM user to IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)
Rotate existing IAM key immediately
AWS SCPs: enforce 90-day key rotation policy organization-wide
MFA enforcement for all human IAM users in PCI-scoped accounts
CloudTrail alert on key usage from unexpected IP or IAM entity

attack_scenarios.name: Multi-Stage Trading Account Takeover + Unauthorized Trading
attack_scenarios.description: Attacker sends spear phishing to senior trader with DocuSign-spoofed link. Trader clicks, Attacker steals session cookie. Attacker uses Redis AUTH bypass to hijack session. Executes 50 unauthorized high-value trades before detection. Covers tracks by deleting CloudTrail events.

attack_scenarios.name: GCP Metadata SSRF to Spanner Admin via Next.js
attack_scenarios.description: Attacker exploits Next.js CVE-2024-34351 SSRF via crafted Host header to steal GCP service account token from metadata server. Uses token to call Spanner Admin API. Modifies trade ledger records to erase evidence of market manipulation, then covers tracks in Cloud Audit Logs.
attack_scenarios.regulatory_impact: SEC enforcement action + criminal fraud charges
